label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to work through this question step by step. The question is asking why the text includes a lot of figures or numbers. The options are (A) record the valuable data, (B) provide concrete evidence, (C) introduce a research method, and (D) give a detailed description. First, I need to understand what the text is about. The text is talking about economic reforms in China, specifically regarding credit cards. It mentions the growth rate of credit card adoption, percentages of households owning credit cards, and the findings of Rui Yao, an assistant professor. The text includes several statistics like "about 30 percent of Chinese urban households now own at least one credit card," "the growth rate of credit card adoption has been an average of 40 percent per year," "more than 90 percent of non-credit card users were unaware of safety issues," "72 percent of Chinese credit card holders were never late on their payments," etc. Now, the question is why these figures are used. Let's look at each option: (A) Record the valuable data: This seems a bit vague. Recording data is a general statement, but the context here is about using figures in the text. The text is not just keeping a record but using the data for a specific purpose. (B) Provide concrete evidence: This makes sense because the figures are used to back up the claims made by Rui Yao. For example, when he says there are positive and negative implications, he supports these statements with specific percentages about credit card usage and knowledge among users. The numbers serve as evidence for the arguments presented in the text. (C) Introduce a research method: The text doesn't explain how the research was conducted. There's no mention of surveys, experiments, data collection methods, etc. The figures are presented as results, not as part of explaining the method. (D) Give a detailed description: While the text does provide details about the growth of credit card usage and the issues found, the main purpose of the figures is to support the points being made rather than just to describe in detail. The statistics are used to substantiate the implications and findings discussed, which is more about evidence than just
